coredata.dc:title: Toward fast charge extraction in all-inorganic CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells by setting intermediate energy levels
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Guoqing
Duan, Jialong
Zhao, Yuanyuan
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: Perovskite-structured cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr3) halide is a rising light harvester for perovskite solar cell applications due to its high absorption coefficient and stability upon heat and humidity attacks. However, the serious electron-hole recombination arising from large interfacial energy differences has markedly limited charge extraction for enhanced photovoltaic performances. Herein, we systematically study fast charge extraction at TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces by setting intermediate energy levels with carbon quantum dots and red phosphorus quantum dots, respectively. The interfacial radiative or trap-dominant recombination can be significantly suppressed owing to the boosted charge transfer ability as well as the passivation effect by incorporating the carbon quantum dots and red phosphorus quantum dots into solar cells. Finally, the optimal perovskite solar cell device achieves a maximized power conversion efficiency of 8.20% under one sun illumination in comparison with 6.37% for quantum dots-free solar cell. Moreover, the efficiency is reduced by only 3% upon persistent attack with 80% relative humidity over 1000 h, suggesting a good environmental tolerance to boost the commercialization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inorganic perovskite solar cells
Charge extraction
Interfacial engineering
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of all-inorganic CsPbBr3 solar cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The illustration for preparing CQDs and RPQDs tailored all-inorganic CsPbBr3 PSC device as well as (b) top-view SEM images of m-TiO2, C element mapping image, top-view SEM images of PbBr2 and CsPbBr3, P element mapping image and carbon layers. TEM images of (c) RPQDs and (d) CQDs.

(a) Cross-sectional SEM image of an all-inorganic PSC device with configuration of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs/carbon. (b) Structural diagram of the inorganic PSC device. (c) Crystal structure of CsPbBr3 halide and (d) XRD pattern of FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3. (e) Charge-transfer processes within a solar cell.

(a) Photo J-V curves and (b) IPCE spectra of pristine, CQDs-only and CQDs/RPQDs tailored all-inorganic PSCs. (c) Statistical histogram of PCEs obtained from 10 individual PSCs. (d) Stable PCE outputs of all-inorganic PSCs devices with configurations of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CsPbBr3/carbon, FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/carbon, and FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs/carbon.

(a) PL and (b) TRPL decay spectra of TiO2/CsPbBr3, TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3 and TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs films. The plots of (c) J sc and (d) V oc as a function of light intensity for three all-inorganic PSCs.

(a) Photo J-V curves of the all-inorganic PSC with FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs/carbon architecture before and after 1056 h operation in 80% RH. Normalized (b) PCE, (c) J sc, (d) V oc and (e) FF for pristine, CQDs-only and CQDs/RPQDs PSCs.
The photovoltaic data of all-inorganic PSCs.

head.ce:title: Toward fast charge extraction in all-inorganic CsPbBr3 perovskite solar cells by setting intermediate energy levels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Laboratory for Deep Blue Fishery Engineering, Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology, Qingdao 266237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces of all-inorganic PSC are modified by CQDs and RPQDs to extract electrons and holes, respectively.

Perovskite-structured cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr3) halide is a rising light harvester for perovskite solar cell applications due to its high absorption coefficient and stability upon heat and humidity attacks. However, the serious electron-hole recombination arising from large interfacial energy differences has markedly limited charge extraction for enhanced photovoltaic performances. Herein, we systematically study fast charge extraction at TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces by setting intermediate energy levels with carbon quantum dots and red phosphorus quantum dots, respectively. The interfacial radiative or trap-dominant recombination can be significantly suppressed owing to the boosted charge transfer ability as well as the passivation effect by incorporating the carbon quantum dots and red phosphorus quantum dots into solar cells. Finally, the optimal perovskite solar cell device achieves a maximized power conversion efficiency of 8.20% under one sun illumination in comparison with 6.37% for quantum dots-free solar cell. Moreover, the efficiency is reduced by only 3% upon persistent attack with 80% relative humidity over 1000 h, suggesting a good environmental tolerance to boost the commercialization.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cell (PSC) is considered as a promising competitor in photovoltaic market (Kojima et al., 2009; Lee et al., 2012; Burschka et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2014), arising from attractive power conversion efficiency (PCE) up to >22% and cost-effectiveness in comparison to silicon solar cells (Yang et al., 2017; Correa-Baena et al., 2017). The excellent performances of hybrid PSCs arise from strong light absorption, weakly bound excitons and long range charge carrier diffusion of methylammonium lead triiodide (MAPbX3) or formamidinium lead triiodide (FAPbX3) halides. A challenging problem for these organic-inorganic hybrid light-harvesting materials are crystal destruction upon persistent moisture and heat attacks (Wang et al., 2017; Salado et al., 2016; Conings et al., 2015). Despite the rapid increase of PCE associated with development of new materials and fabrication techniques, the application of state-of-the-art hole-transporting layers (HTL, e.g, spiro-OMeTAD) and noble metals (e.g, Au) still markedly increase solar cell cost for potential commercialization (Duan et al., 2017).

One possible solution to this impasse is to replace organic MA+ or FA+ species with inorganic Cs+ ion and substituting I− with Br−. The resultant inorganic CsPbX3 (X = I, Br) halides are featured with remarkable stability against heat and humidity, comparable light absorption ability and high carrier mobility (Chen et al., 2017; Rong et al., 2015; Liang et al., 2017). Therefore, all-inorganic CsPbBr3 solar cells with classical configuration of FTO/TiO2/CsPbBr3/carbon have inevitably attracted considerable attentions owing to their excellent long-term stability (Liang et al., 2016). However, the corresponding solar-to-electrical conversion ability is inferior to that of hybrid devices although substitution one or two Br− ions with I− species may markedly increase cell performances by sacrificing the heat and humidity tolerances. How to enhance the PCE of inorganic CsPbBr3 solar cell is an urgent objective to promote commercialization. For all state-of-the-art PSCs, the serious charge-carrier trap state within solar cells including perovskite films and interfaces limits the further improvement of photovoltaic performance. Taking interfacial charge transfer into consideration, reducing interfacial energy-level differences and passivating interfacial defect provide possibility to reduce space charge accumulation and enhance charge extraction (Chang et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017; Lau et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2017).

Quantum dots are semiconductor particles with several nanometers, size-dependent optical and electronic behaviors and tunable energy-level structures. Additionally, the small size allows quantum dots to be suspended in solutions which leads to be used in solution-processable inorganic PSC devices. Wide bandgap windows, high molar extinction coefficient and electron mobility make carbon quantum dots (CQDs) ideal for buffer materials at TiO2 (−4.2 eV)/CsPbBr3 (−3.3 eV) interface. By designing CQDs with the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy between −4.2 eV and −3.3 eV, photogenerated electrons can be extracted from conduction band (CB) of CsPbBr3 to CQDs and subsequently transfer to percolating TiO2 pathways. Similarly, red phosphorus quantum dots (RPQDs) having the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) at −5.6 to −5.0 eV and high hole mobility are used to set an intermediate energy level to extract photo-induced holes. Arising from the suppressed interfacial charge recombination, the optimal device yields a PCE up to 8.20% under one sun illumination, which is much higher than that of QD-free device. The current work mainly studies charge extraction processes by interfacial engineering and reveals potential mechanism behind promoted electron-hole separation.

The RPQDs were prepared through an ultrasound-assistant technology. In details, bulky red phosphorus was grinded into ultrafine powders and for ultrasonic dispersion in 50 mL of 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) to obtain homogeneously red phosphorus solution. Then, the resultant dispersion was transferred into a three-necked round-bottomed flask and heated at 150 °C for 4 h under vigorous agitation in nitrogen atmosphere. After cooling to room temperature, the RPQDs were obtained by filtering and centrifuging for 20 min at 7000 rpm and finally re-dispersed in methylbenzene for use.

CQDs were synthesized by a mild bottom-up route with polyethylene glycol (PEG, M w = 600) as a carbon source. Briefly, 10 mL of PEG was heated to 230 °C for 1 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The products were dispersed in acetone, and then centrifugated and rinsed for several times. Finally, the resultant CQDs powders were collected and further dispersed in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) for use.

An FTO glass was etched using 2.0 M HCl aqueous solution and Zn powders for desirable patterns and then thoroughly rinsed with acetone, ethanol and deionized water. Subsequently, a thin layer of compact TiO2 (c-TiO2) layer was deposited on the FTO glass by spin-coating an ethanol solution of titanium isopropoxide (0.5 M) and diethanol amine (0.5 M) at 7000 rpm for 30 s, and annealed at 500 °C for 2 h in air. The mesoscopic TiO2 (m-TiO2) layer was deposited onto c-TiO2 layer by spin-coating colloidal TiO2 paste (Hu et al., 2014) at 2000 rpm for 30 s and annealed at 450 °C for 30 min in air. Afterwards, the FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2 photoanode was immersed in a 40 mM of TiCl4 aqueous solution at 75 °C for 30 min. After being rinsed with deionized water and ethanol, the photoanode was further calcined at 450 °C for another 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of all-inorganic CsPbBr3 solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: All the processes for building CsPbBr3 based inorganic PSC devices were conducted in ambient air (10–20 °C, 30–60 RH%). In details, 90 µL of CQD solution in DMF was spin-coated onto the FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2 layer at 2000 rpm for 30 s and heated at 90 °C for 5 min. Afterwards, a DMF solution of 1.0 M PbBr2 was spin-coated onto the CQDs layer at 2000 rpm for 30 s, followed by dried at 90 °C for 30 min. Then, 90 µL of CsBr solution in methanol was spin-coated on FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CQDs/PbBr2 at 2000 rpm for 30 s and heated to 250 °C for 5 min on a hotplate. High-purity CsPbBr3 layer was made by repeatedly spin-coating and drying CsBr layer for five times. Subsequently, freshly prepared RPQD methylbenzene solution was spin-coated onto CsPbBr3 film at 2000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, commercial carbon paste was coated at RPQD layer by a doctor-blade method and heated at 90 °C for 10 min. Notably, CQDs/RPQDs-free and CQDs-only PSC devices were also made according to the similar procedures.

The photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves of all-inorganic PSCs were studied on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation. The solar simulator equipped with a 100 W xenon arc lamp and the incident light intensity was controlled at 100 mW cm−2 by calibrating with a standard silicon solar cell. The incident-photo-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra were characterized by a power source (Newport 300 W xenon lamp, 66920) with amonochromator (Newport Cornerstone 260) in the wavelength range of 300–850 nm at room temperature. The obtained crystal structure of CsPbBr3 film was examined by an X-ray diffraction (PHILIPSPW1800 diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation). The morphologies of the solar cell layers and total PSC device were characterized by a field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Japan Hitachi field emission SU8220). The absorption spectra of CQDs and RPQDs were measured using an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu). The time-resolved PL measurement was assessed by time-resolved fluorescence (Horiba Jobin Yvon, FL).

Fig. 1 a illustrates the assembly processes of all-inorganic PSC devices by a solution-processable method and the corresponding top-view SEM or mapping images are shown in Fig. 1b. In briefs, c-TiO2 and m-TiO2 layer with 400 nm in thickness is spin-coated onto FTO glass and then calcined in air to obtain high-quality anatase phase. Subsequently, CQD solution is coated onto m-TiO2 layer surface to obtain a homogeneous distribution, which can be cross-checked by the C element mapping in Fig. 1b. The CsPbBr3 perovskite film is made through a multi-step solution-processed by spin-coating a layer of PbBr2 and successively four layers of CsBr developed by Tang (Duan et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 1b, the as-prepared perovskite film is composed with separated grains with average size of 290 nm. To realize the interfacial modification, a RPQD toluene solution is spin-coated onto the surface of CsPbBr3 film, obtaining a homogeneous distribution as shown in Fig. 1b, which is expected to facilitate charge extraction during the practical application. After spin-coating RPQD solution, commercial carbon paste composing of graphene and carbon black is coated onto the surface of FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CsPbBr3/RPQD by a doctor-blade method and then dried under 90 °C for 5 min to make carbon electrode (Fig. 1b). It should be noted that the corresponding QDs cannot be discovered in SEM images owing to their smaller size and low dosages. To better understand the properties of the as-prepared RPQDs and CQDs, the TEM characterizations are performed as shown in Fig. 1c and d, showing homogeneous distribution and average sizes of 12.1 and 7 nm, respectively. The PL spectra of RPQDs and CQDs under excitation wavelengths from 330 to 510 nm (shown in Fig. S1a and b) which is common for the fluorescent carbon and phosphorus materials. The light absorption spectra of RPQDs (emit green light under excitation wavelength of 365 nm) and CQDs (emit blue light under excitation wavelength of 365 nm) are recorded as shown in Fig. S1c, indicating their excellent optical properties. The UV-vis absorption spectrum of CsPbBr3 layer in Fig. S2 indicates a narrow absorption range below 540 nm, giving a bandgap of 2.3 eV. The presence of large energy differences between conduction band of TiO2 (−4.2 eV) and that of CsPbBr3 (−3.3 eV), as well as the valence band of CsPbBr3 (−5.6 eV) and work function of carbon (−5.0 eV) has produced serious electron-hole recombination and therefore degrading the photovoltaic performance. Therefore, the intermediate energy levels by setting CQDs and RPQDs at solar cell interfaces are expected to boost charge extraction for reduced electron-hole recombination. In this scenario, the energy architectures of as-prepared QDs are crucial to realize the purpose. To better understand the energy levels of CQDs and RPQDs, a commonly used cyclic voltammetry (CV) method is performed in acetonitrile solution containing 0.1 M of tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6) to determine their energy-level structures according to following equations:
(1)
E HOMO
= - e
E ox
+ 4.4
V
(2)
E LOMO
= - e
E red
+ 4.4
V
where E ox and E red are the onset potentials of oxidation and reduction peaks, respectively (Gupta et al., 2011). According to the CV curves in Figs. S1d and S1e, the E red and E ox of RPQDs are −0.31 and 0.69 V, assigning to LUMO and HOMO levels of −4.1 and −5.1 eV, respectively. Similarly, the LUMO of CQDs is determined to be −3.4 eV and the HOMO value is −5.6 eV. In this fashion, the matching energy-level structures of CQDs and RPQDs make them promising in extracting electrons and holes at TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces, respectively.

Fig. 2 a shows a cross-sectional SEM image of the total all-inorganic PSC with FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs/carbon configuration, giving a well-contacted multilayer and obvious interfaces. CQDs and RPQDS are not observed in the cross section because they are homogeneous distributed at top layers instead of forming films. In this fashion, the corresponding all-inorganic PSCs are HTL-free using RPQDs as hole extraction materials. Fig. 2b illustrates the solar cell structure and charges transfer according to paths in Fig. 2e. Upon sunlight illumination, the CsPbBr3 layer absorbs photons and releases electron-hole pairs. The photo-induced electrons transfer to CQDs and TiO2 under local electric field, leaving holes to RPQDs and then carbon electrode. Perovskite-structured CsPbBr3 is crucial for high-performance solar cells, in which A sites are occupied by Cs+ ions, B sites are occupied by Pb2+ ions, and X sites are occupied by Br− ions (Fig. 2c) (Choi et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2015; Yi et al., 2016; Saliba et al., 2016a; Saliba et al., 2016b). The crystal structure of CsPbBr3 is subjected to X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization, as shown in Fig. 2d, the detection of diffraction patterns at 2θ = 15.3° and 21.7° without other cesium lead bromide phases means high-purity CsPbBr3 layer has been made using multi-step solution-processed method reported here.

Fig. 3 a shows the photocurrent-voltage (J-V) curves of all-inorganic PSCs under one sun illumination and the corresponding photovoltaic parameters including short-circuit current density (J sc), open-circuit voltage (V oc), fill factor (FF) and PCE are summarized in Table 1 . The pristine CsPbBr3 solar cell yields a PCE of 6.37% (V oc = 1.276 V, J sc = 6.66 mA cm−2, FF = 0.75), which is comparable to the state-of-the-art HTL-free all-inorganic PSCs. By setting an intermediate energy level with CQDs at TiO2/CsPbBr3 interface, the PCE increases to 7.14% with higher J sc, V oc and FF outputs. The mechanism behind this enhancement is the reduction of interfacial energy difference between conduction band (CB) of TiO2 (−4.2 eV) and CsPbBr3 (−3.4 eV) and therefore boosting the charge extraction ability from perovskite. Upon further modification CsPbBr3/carbon interface with RPQDs, the final all-inorganic PSC achieves a PCE as high as 8.20%, which is 28.7% higher than that of the reference device. Obviously, V oc J sc, and FF are also elevated to 1.470 V, 7.33 mA cm−2 and 0.76, respectively. Till now, we can conclude that the interfacial engineering is an effective strategy to enhance photovoltaic performances by tuning carrier extraction processes. The incident photo-to-electric current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra of various solar cells are used to cross-check the photoelectric conversion behaviors, as shown in Fig. 3b. Due to the large bandgap of CsPbBr3 halide, all the solar cells have narrow light-response at λ < 550 nm, which is accordance with the UV–visible light absorbance spectra in Fig. S2. The maximal IPCE values for pristine, CQDs-only and CQDs/RPQDs based solar cells are 86%, 89% and 91%, respectively. Compared with the pristine cell, the introduction of CQDs and RPQDs with a lower LUMO and slightly higher HUMO at the TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces accelerate the charge transfer, leading to the improved photoelectric conversion ability. The CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs device shows the best IPCE performance among the three cells, which is accordance with its highest J sc. Integrating the product of AM 1.5 G with the IPCE spectrum, the corresponding J sc are 6.58, 6.66 and 7.23 mA cm−2 which is in agreement with the measured value from the J-V scanning. In order to demonstrate the reproducibility of these inorganic PSCs, random 20 cells are measured and the PCE distribution is shown in Fig. 3c. The narrow PCE distributions and high PCE values suggest high-performance all-inorganic PSC devices with good reproducibility can be made using the method reported here. To cross-check the validity of the above-mentioned conclusion, the stable power output characterizations are performed at their maximal power points as shown in Fig. 3d. All the solar cells have stabilized current outputs over 110 s and the corresponding PCEs are found to be 6.33%, 6.89% and 7.99%, which is in comparison with pristine, CQDs-only and CQDs/RPQDs solar cells in J-V characterizations. The efficiency deviations may be caused by hysteresis effect. Although the cause of J-V hysteresis is not fully understood, stable power output and bias corresponding to the maximum power point under continuous lighting are accepted as standard procedures. To investigate the photocurrent hysteresis effect of the as-fabricated pristine, CQDs-only and CQDs/RPQDs solar cells, J-V curves with different scan directions were recorded and presented in Fig. S3. Difference in PCE between the backward and forward scans (Difference factor = (ƞ backward − ƞ forkward)/ƞ backward) is as large as 11.59%, which is reduced to 0.62% for CQDs/RPQDs tailored PSC. The J-V hysteresis is related to capacitive current in the full devices. The origin of the capacitive current has been studied using a variety of techniques, from which possible be related to the device architecture (Lee et al., 2017). Therefore, we can reduce the hysteresis effect by introducing QDs with appropriate energy level structure as interfacial modification layer to greatly improve the device performance.

To further understand the intrinsic mechanism behind the performance enhancement, spectral characterizations including steady-state PL and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra are performed to study the interfacial charge recombination processes. Pristine CsPbBr3 film has a sharp PL peak centered at 535 nm (Fig. 4 a), while the peak intensities are quenched by 29% and 57% without changing peak position by incorporating CQDs and CQDs/RPQDs into the interfaces, respectively. Taking the photoluminescence production process into consideration, the photogenerated electrons and holes are expected to combine maximally to yield a high PL peak intensity. In this fashion, the quenched characteristic peak can be attributed to the reduced recombination probability within perovksite film, resulting from the boosted charge extraction behavior upon interfacial modification with CQDs and RPQDs owing to their reduced energy difference (Zhou and Yin, 2017; Tang et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2014). From the TRPL spectroscopy in Fig. 4b, we can obtain the charge carrier lifetimes according to biexponential decay model: (Dong et al., 2018)
I
t
=
A 1
exp
- t /
τ 1
+
A 2
exp
- t /
τ 2
where the shorter lifetime (τ 1) and longer lifetime (τ 2) are mainly assigned to the trap-mediated nonradiative recombination and radiative recombination. The charge carrier lifetimes of the CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs perovskite film is determined to τ 1 = 68.9 ± 6.20 ps, τ 2 = 1.26 ± 0.09 ns, and the charge carrier lifetimes of the CQDs/CsPbBr3 perovskite film is τ 1 = 94.9 ± 4.5 ps, τ 2 = 2.39 ± 0.18 ns, which are much shorter than that (τ 1 = 151.6 ± 6.0 ps and τ 2 = 3.39 ± 0.08 ns) of the pure CsPbBr3 perovskite film. The charge carrier lifetimes represent the ability of carrier extraction for these three type devices structure. Clearly, the PL quenching in the CQDs/CsPbBr3 perovskite film and CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs perovskite film are faster than that of CsPbBr3 perovskite film, which is consistent with our above-mentioned conclusion. Moreover, the CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs presents higher electron extraction efficiency than that of CQDs/CsPbBr3. Aside from the reduced trap-state density, we assume it likely attributes to the well matching of gradient energy band between the perovskite layer and QDs, which favors the photo-generated electron to be extracted from perovskite layer to carbon electrode effectively. Fig. 4c shows the dependence of J sc on light intensity for three all-inorganic PSCs, suggesting a dominant bimolecular mechanism in solar cells (Jiang et al., 2016). The larger slopes (α) of 0.98 for CQDs-only solar cell and 0.99 for CQDs/RPQDs PSC in comparison with 0.96 for QDs-free device indicates that bimolecular recombination is suppressed due to fast charge-extracting effects from CQDs and RPQDs. The light intensity dependence of the V oc of three all-inorganic PSCs is shown in Fig. 4d. The dependence of V oc output on light intensity reveals the degree of trap-dominated recombination. The slopes of 1.72 k B T/q, 1.60 k B T/q and 1.22 k B T/q (where k B refers to the Boltzmann constant, T means absolute temperature, and q is elementary charge) are obtained for QDs-free, CQDs-only and CQDs/RPQDs based PSCs, respectively. In principle, the slope is equal to k B T/q if the device does not have charge trapping or is governed by bimolecular recombination. The slope greater than k B T/q is an indicator that trap-dominated monmolecular recombination is involved in the device operation, which refers to any first-order process including the geminate recombination of a bound electron-hole pair before dissociation and the Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH) recombination at the shallow traps created by defects and impurities in the interfacial layer. In this fashion, the smaller slopes for CQDs/RPQDs (1.22 k B T/q) and CQDs-only (1.60 k B T/q) solar cells than 1.72 k B T/q for pristine device confirm a markedly reduced unimolecular SRH recombination, which may be attributed to the passivation effect after inserting CQDs and RPQDs into the TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces (Zhang et al., 2017). All of these characterizations illustrate the improved charge extraction by modifying cell interfaces with CQDs and RPQDs in all-inorganic PSCs.

High efficiency and improved stability are prerequisites to promote commercialization of PSC platforms. To study the long-term stability of our all-inorganic PSC with configuration of FTO/TiO2/CQDs/CsPbBr3/RPQDs/carbon, the device is completely exposed to ambient air with 80% relative humidity. The photovoltaic parameters including PCE, J sc, V oc and FF are measured over 1056 h. Fig. 5 a shows the photo J-V curves before and after 1056 h under humid air, no obvious changes are found in shapes and photovoltaic behaviors. The PCEs remain 98%, 95% and 97% for pristine, CQDs-only and CQDs/RPQDs PSCs (Fig. 5b), respectively. By comparing other data such as J sc, V oc and FF, there is no evident deviations detected in Fig. 5c–e. Till now, we can make a conclusion that cost-effective, high efficiency and stable all-inorganic PSCs can be made through setting intermediate energy levels at interfaces by a multi-step solution-processed method.

In summary, we launch a multi-step solution-processable method for all-inorganic CsPbBr3 solar cell, and the PCE is improved through setting intermediate energy levels at TiO2/CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3/carbon interfaces with CQDs and RPQDs, respectively. Due to fast charge extraction and passivation effect, a maximum PCE as high as 8.20% is achieved for optimized PSC in comparison with 6.37% for pristine device. Moreover, our all-inorganic PSCs are also highlighted by high stability even in 80% relative humidity over 1056 h. The interfacial engineering study on all-inorganic PSCs provides new opportunities of enhancing charge extraction and therefore photovoltaic conversion efficiency.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (61774139, 21503202) and Director Foundation from Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology (QNLM201702).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2018.06.041.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sequential deposition as a route to high-performance perovskite-sensitized solar cells
Carbon-based CsPbBr 3 perovskite solar cells: all-ambient processes and high thermal stability
All-vacuum-deposited stoichiometrically balanced inorganic cesium lead halide perovskite solar cells with stabilized efficiency exceeding 11%
Cesium-doped methylammonium lead iodide perovskite light absorber for hybrid solar cells
Intrinsic thermal instability of methylammonium lead trihalide perovskite
